CGURRERT AFFAIRS,

Mr. Michacl Meaghor, M.P., delivored a ‘“ very stir-
ring address’’ to his constituents in Kilkenny.  Mr.
Meagher, the Irish National patriot, voted in favour of
Mr. Bung on the division on the Bill brought in by
~ Mr. Sloan, the Orangeman. “In the first place,” said
the eloquent Meagher, “ho believed that before tho
English people should attempt to teach them a lesson
they should practise it at home.”” Mr, Meagher went
‘on to refer to the long hours during which bungerics
wero open on Sundays in London. “He saw more
drunken men, in one night in London than he did in
‘his life in Ireland.”” Evidently it would be a good thing
~ if the bungeries were shut up earlier there. And surely
would be a good thing to shut Irish drunkeries earlier

an intelloctual acrobat. Though in London of the long
hours Mr. Meagher saw more drunken men in one night
than ho had seen all his life in Ireland, yet if they shut
up tho Kilkenny publichouses—we wonder has Mr.
Meaghor any interest in the trade—at 9 o’clock at night,
‘“ the young men of the city of Kilkenny and other cities
would form themselves into a club and remain jn the
club not only up to ten o’clock or eleven o’clock, but all
night, for the sum of 2s. 6d. a year, and they would be
not only robbing and depriving the publicans of their
way of living, but they would be endangering the so-
briety of the people. He lived three Irish miles away
from any village, and he certainly did not like to pay
his men before 7 o’clock, and they would not arrive at a
publichouse before nine. By this means shebeening
would be increased, and intemperance would be in-
creased, and instead of drinking honestly at the counter
tho people would be drinking in the ditches, shebeening
houses and other places.”

Dan Tallon, we should say, would be nowhere in the
Button pressing line in comy 1 with the eloquent
Meagher of Kilkenny. Drinking in the ditches! Surely .
Mr. Meagher, to combat this dreadful poszibility, ought .
to agitate for the establishment of a licensed bungery
beside every ditch in the country! And surely, in view
of the possibility of young men in Kilkenny staying up
all night in clubs, and so robbing poor Mr. Bung of his
way of living, the eloquent Mr. Meagher should bring
in a Bill authorising the Bungs of Kilkenny at least to
remain open permanently. If Bung’s Bond Slaves ob-
jected to these hours, perhaps Mr. Meagher could ar-
rango for the introduction of Chinese labour for the
Bungeries of Kilkenny. We cannot conscientiously say,
judging from the eloquent gentleman’s pronouncement
on Bung, that the Mr. Meagher, M.P., is an acquisition
to the Irish Parliamentary Party.

Lord Castletown, the “ Grand Secretary ”’ of the per-
nicious secret society, the Free and Accepted Masons of
Ireland, has been appointed Chancellor of the Royal
University of Ireland. We read that the King ap-
pointed him by warrant under the Royal Sign Manual.
He could not have been appointed in a more appropriate
way. The King is patron of this pernicious secret so-
ciety, in which “the sign’’ is said to be so important.
Ono of the paid secretaries of the Royal University, Sir
J. Creod Meredith, is “ Deputy Grand Master ’’ of the
Freemasons in Ireland, and probably he worked the
Lodges in favour of the appointment of Brother Castle-
town, the “Grand Secretary.”” Now that these two
prominent, Freemasons are in high positions in the Royal
University the brethren should give the “sign ’ to get
a place on the Senate for Brother Goulding, gentleman,
the “ Grand Steward ’’ of the Frocmasons in Ireland.

At a fete in connection with a sale of work for the
bonefit of the Protestant Church in Newtownards, Lord
Londonderry made a speech in which he said : “ He need
only tell them that in accordance with the traditions of
his house ho was ready at all times to further any case
for the bencfit of the people.” The “traditions’’ of
the house of Castlereagh suggest curious reflections to
the Irish reader. '

‘What is the use of the ““Liberal '’ Government talk-
ing raimeis about governing Ireland according to Ivish
ideas in view—to mention only one item—of the
seizure of a ton of coal belonging to a member of the
Dublin Corporation, Paopais O Ceapdail because he re~
fused to pay a fine of 10s. for the “crime” of having his
name in Irish only on his cart? Mr. Bryce, who must be ]

a very wily, or a very simple, old man, has been ex-
pressing “ sympathy ’’ with the Gaelic League, and yet
Dublin Castle fines a man 10s. for the “ crime ’’ wo have
. mentioned, and seizes a ton of his coals when he vory
properly refuses to pay the unjust exaction. Dublin
Castle is a very big sediment, and it will take a lot of
removing. The Dublin Main Drainage, which is not
yet completed, is only a bagatelle compared with the
work of draining Dublin Castle. The Government is
dead; long live—Dublin Castle.  Dublin Castlo is a
parmanent official, and permanent officials arc sticky
fellows, though they grow lazy and bumptious in soft,
well-paid jobs.

What Nationalists will have to do is to get hold of
the Positions of power in this country. That is one of
the chief works before Sinn Feén. Dublin Castle
really means this, and nothing more—the Sourfaces in
power all over the country. And unfortunately there
s a great deal of muddied thought concerning this
matter. As wo believe that no people can do our own
business but ourselves—that is to say, the LEADER or
Sinn - Pémn  idea—we see that no satisfactory workiug
arrangement of any kind can be arrived at until the
Nationalists are in power instead of the anti-Irish Sour-
faces—be they Cawtholic or Profestant. British Govern-
ments may change, but Dublin Castle goes on so long
as th> machinery is in the hands of the Sowfaces; if
the Nationalists had the machinery, British Govern-
ments might come and go, but it would be a matter
of comparative indifference to us—pending Home Rule
—which Government was in power.

At a recent weekly meeting of the Carrickmacross
Union, Mr. Dwyer moved thati a memorial be sent to
the Lo:d Chancellor praying that one Mr. James
Murphy be made a Justice of the Peace for the County
of Monaghan. Mr. Dwyer is a local Bung who refused
to close his shop on St. Patrick’s Day. We understand
that the local Bungs in general as well as Mr. Dwyer in
particular are anxious to have Mr. Murphy on the
Bench; and that in ifself is a strong reason why he
should not be on it. The making of magistrates costs
no money, and it is conceivable that the “ Liberal ’’
Government may try to gain a little popularity by mak-
ing “ Nationalist *’ magistrates with one hand, while it
squeezes the Irish language with the other. It would
be well for the friends of Temperance to keep a sharp
look out for any developments in the J.P.-making direc-
tion. Bung would like to have magistrates who can be
depended upon to take a pro-Bung view of things; the
country would be the better of it if all the magistrates
were at least not afraid of Bung. If Monaghan re-
quires a new magistrate we trust that the country will
at least not bo saddled with a nominee of Mr. Bung.

““One of ‘ Slatfery’s Mounted Fut’ ’” writes : — Dear
Sir,—In your issue of the 9th inst. your correspondent
¢ Chanel ’ quotes a passage from Mr. T. O. Russell’s book
in which it is stated that formerly the National Bank had
a majority of ‘Saved’ on its staff, and that at the pre-
sent time ‘ Idolators’ and ‘Saved’ are about equal.
This is not the case. I doubt if over there was a
“Saved ’ majority on the staff, and know for a fact that
a the present time the ‘ Idolators ’ constitute somewhat
more than two-thirds of the entire staff. Chairman and
Vice-Chairman both Catholics, Both joint managers
in Dublin are Catholics, as is also the sub-manager.”

The Aonach at Pockham, London, was a groab succoss.
It was held in the Marist grounds, Peckham, and be-
sides being of great help in the work of popularising
TIrish good:s in London, was a particular financial aid to
the Franciscan Capuchins at Peckham. Tho Aonach
was opered by the Dowager Countess Desart, who was
so_prominently identified with tho establishing of the
Kilkenny Woollen Milis. Father Elzoar, 0.8.F.0,, in
the course of his speech, said:—Mr, Corrigan, one of
the heads of a well-known London Irish firm (Messrs.
Corrigan and Fronch, Aldersgate Street), had put the

fore try to push our Irish manufactured goods. - Hun-

to America and other counfries, because they cannot

At the Belfast Recorder’s Court a grocer sued one
Morris Norrick, a Jew. We are not interested in {he
merits of the case, and we only refer to it as we se that
Morris Norrick stated in the course of the hearing of it
that “ We are not allowed to take food got from a
Christian’s shop.””  Well, evidently they have full
power to take instalments from Turk, Heathen cr Chris-
tian.

Mr. H. T. Barrie, M.P., was one of the spokesmen
at the Presbyterian General Assembly. In the course
of his remarks he said :—“ He would suggest that the
congregations should always make it a point of choosing
the man with the cheeriest countenance to stand at the
church door on the Sabbath, a man not timid to shake
hands with people he did not know, a man who was
prepared to take the risk of a rebuff, a man who would
bid strangers welcome and ask them to come again and
mako them feel that the Church had a real and living
interest in their welfare.”” Ts this not rather a back-
hander at the real, literal, Sourfaces? The cheeryfaces
in the Black North ought to be in brisk demand, for
the supply is somewhat limited we should say.

A correspondent writes:—“ W
of the agricultural machinery v at the Department’s
agricultural stations?’ In the Midlands the writer can
answer this question for last year up to November. Till
then not less than ninety-eight per cent. were imported.
But what most disgusted Industrial Revivalists was the
travelling exhibition of imported churns hawked round
by tho itinerant instructresses on butter making. The
resul{s that any ordinary person could foresee at the time
were inevitable—and all the farmers’ daughters in
tho country went crazy after these churns. Will you
turn your attention, Mr. Editor, to the Gaelic Athletic
Association, and say what is the nationality of the hun-
dreds of prizes given at athletic spovts all over the coun-
try under the rules of the G.A.A.? Do these rules
malko it obligatory that these prizes must be Irish manu-
facture? Do the rules of the Gaelic, or Irish Cycling
Association encourage competitors to competo on Irish-
mado machinies?  Tho season for Gaclic sports
and prize winning is now on, and in the Midlands
especially tho great Multifarnham Franciscan Bazaar
has given the lead in the prizes as to the nationality
they ;s]muld be.  Will the G.A.A. and the I.C.A. fol-
low ?*

at is the nationality

The Convent of the Sacred Heart, Ferrybank, Water-
ford, was recenfly tho sceno of a Fois in miniature.
Very Rov. Canon Phelan presided, and Canon Holahan
presonbed several prizes.  Father Delany, C.C\., and
Tather Bergin, C.0., adjudicated. The programme in-
cluded tests in the Irish language, music, history and

" Messrs. Forrest and Sons, Ltd., a Grafton Street In-
ternational Exhibition shop, took it into their heads to
send to our private address a circular and a printed
letter. We gather from the printed letter that Messrs.
Forrest havo secured the services of “an expert French
cutter and fitter ”—another importation; and this
printed announcement is made on paper with the water-
mark, ‘“antient Scotbish vellum.” = Wo suspect very
strongly that Messes. Forrest aro guarantors for Hum-
phrey’s Dump.

One Mr. Easthope, an Englishman, came to the West,
He was imported into Dundalk in 1901 by the local
Technical Committeo. The Department sanctioned his
appointment. Like Brother Goulding, Mr. Easthopo is,
we understand, a Freemason.  Though English and
though sanctioned by the Department, Mr. Easthope
e did not prove an unqualified suceess. Tho teachers of
¢ Cookery and Shorthand happen to be  Idolators,”” and
their paths have been none too smooth. Another “ Ido-
lator "’—we believe he was somewhat timid—uwas teach-
ing applied mechanics and was shifted. If the teachers
don’t show results the Department Inspector is likely
to give them an unfavourable report. The applied me-
chanics class was held at the same hour and on the same
night as the class in steam and kindred subjects, sub-
jects to which students of applied mechanics wero likely
to attend. An unfavourable report of the applied me-
class was made, and the teacher was handed his
walking papers. Though the head master is an Eng-
lishman and though his appointment was approved of by
the Department, he does not seem to have gained the
confidence of tlie teachers or scholars.

Things went so far {hat there was an inquiry into
the complaints at these schools against the IHeadmaster,
notwithstanding that he is an Englishman and was ap-
proved of by the Department. The Inquiry was held in
the Town Hall, Dundalk, before Messrs. T. C. Macardle,
J.P., Chairman of the Technical Committee; B. Hamill,
J.P.; J. M. Johnson, J. P. McGinty, and Ii. O’Con-
nell, J.P. The Englishman was present. A lctter was
handed in by the Englishman, who was sanctioned by
the Department, purporting to be signed by four ladies,
to the effect that they having given a statemont of their
caso to the Press that they wished the matter to be let
drop. One of the young ladies who was supposed to
have signed this epistle, Miss Morgan, declared that she
knew nothing of the letter and that she gave no one
authority to sign her name to it. As the letter and the
signatures happened to be in the same handwriting, the
Committee decided to take no notice of it. Mr, East-
hope, the English importation, stated that the cookery
examination fell through and that the rules of the City
and Guilds required ten students to sit or no examina-
o tion would be held. Mr, Easthope had explained in
writing that some of the students after entry seemed
disinclined 4o sit. A reply signed by four students
stated that “ as a matter of fact there were no students
disinclined to sit for tho exam.—all were anxious to do
s0.”” In view of this conflict of testimony we are in-
clined to give credenco to tho four ladies, notwithstand-
ing that Mr. Easthope is an Englishman and had beon
sanctioned by the Department.

We have somo special information concerning this
business, but wo are also largely indebted to tho excel-
lent report of the Inquiry which appeared in a recenb
issuo of our contemporary, the Dundalk Ezaminer. Wo
‘quote from the Ezaminer’s report:— The chairman
asked Mr, Easthope to produce the regulations of the
City and Guilds. On their being produced it was ascer-
tained that if the classes wero registered in proper time

 that a centro could bo arranged for five students.”
. What becomes of Mr. Easthope’s statoment that tho
rules required len students to sit or no examinabion
would bo held? After some furthor evidenco, ‘M.
Ba,mﬂ] said it was scandalous, and that the Committee

should do all they could to facilitate Miss Morgan and
all young ladies like her.’’

Tho Commibiee subsequently inquired into the'com-
plaints concerning tho shorthand class. Miss Morgan
had some correspondence with Messrs. Pitman and wibh
Mr. Easthope, the English “ expert *’ sanctioned by lthe
Department. ~ Mr. Easthope, the chivalrous English-
man concluded ono of his replies to Miss Morgan with
this sentence: “Tho tone of your letter was imperti-
nent.””  Weo quote from the Dundalk Ezaminer’s re-
port:—*“On tho reading of this letter, the chairman
asked Mr. Basthopo had he written it, and on his (Mr.
Basthope) admitting he had, the chairman said that it
was better that should nover have been written; that
Miss sMorgan was not impertinent, but acted strictly
within her rights.” This Englishman, imported into
Dundalk with tho sanction of the Department, ordered
two patent chemical combined benches from Birming-
ham for Dundalk and Drogheda Schools; perhaps that
was his way of encouraging Irish industry. He ex-
plained that the reason he bought them was to save the
building of an additional room at a cost of £200 in the
new schools that are being built, and the plans and
specifications for which were adopted nearly 12 months
before the benches were thought of. All the young
carpenters left the schools as a protest.

An Irish school-book which has been awaited with
great interest is to appear, wo are told, before the end
of June. It is already known as A&n Mact
by yfeapsup, Fmmnbeit (or Dermot Foley). Mr. Fo
great experience of Irish teaching, before and since his
appointment- to the Munster Training College, and his
study of the best oral methods, make it safe to assume
that his work will prove an invaluable help to learners
and teachers. The publishers are the Irish Book Com-
pany, 6 D’'Olier Street, Dublin.

Mr. Michael Sullivan attended at the Rathmines
Town Hall on Saturday morning last for the purpose of
handing in, in writing, the various objections he made
orally at the opening of the audit on Thursday morning.
However, the auditor was not present! He had gone
somewhere, and would not be back until Tuesday morn-
ing! Perhaps after the proceedings of Thursday the
auditor needed a change, and may have gone for a “rest
cure” to Rathmines-on-Sea, otherwise called Greystones.

Several ratepayers of Rathmines turned into the Town
Hall on Tuesday morning at eleven o’clock in order to
lodge in writing the objections that they had made on
the previous Thursday. The editor of this paper, at
considerable inconvenience, went there, but there was
no auditor.  The auditor arrived at 11.15.  Subse-
quently he referred to the taking up of his time if
lengthened discussions and inquiries were indulged in:
but what about the ratepayers’ time who called on
Saturday to find that the auditor was not in attendance,
and what of the timo of those who called at eleven on
Tuesday and had to wait for a quarter of an hour be-
fore tho auditor arrived, though ho should have been
thero ab ten o’clock? M. Fawsett, of the many jobs,
was not in attendance. We understood the accountant
to say that he was not in the building. Perhaps in his
capacity as paid secretary to the “ust Collection Com-
mittee, or some such momentous job as that, Mr. Faw-
sobt may have been busy elsewhere. Or perhaps he had
cnough of Chamber music on Thursday and did not
want any more on Tuesday. Several written objections
were handed in, and tho roport of M, King
will bo awaited with an interest that does not usually
contre round the labours of Local Government Board
auditors.

It is now over threo years ago that the exposure con-
corning the bigotry on the Great Northern Railway
appeared in the Lraper. The struggle for some share
of Tolerance, Justice and Fair Play for Irish “Idola-

TR

tors” in connection with that Company has been kept up

'in season and out of season in the columns of this paper,
but probably many who once had hopes had begun to
give up the fight as a lost one. At the last half-yearly
meeting of the Company, February 14th, Mr. Joseph
Dolan, of Ardee, as an addition to the report, moved :
“That the Directors be requested to prepare a schome
for throwing entrance to our clerkships open to compe-
titive examination, somewhat after the manner of the
Civil Service and the Great Southern and the Midland
Railways.”” - There were & good many friends of Toler-
ance ab this general meeting, and when the matter was
put to the shout it appeared to many that the “ Ayes
had it, but the chairman, following the example of old
Mr. Edmundson, of the Rathmines Council of bigotry,
declared for his own side, and refused a poll.

‘When several members of Parliament blocked the Bill
being promoted by the Great Northern Company in
consquence of the refusal to throw open its clerkships
to competitive examination a new phase of the struggle
for Tolerance and Fair Play was entered upon.  The
Belfast Northern Whig began to yell like a mad bull.
However, the friends of Tolerance not agreeing to be
intimidated by the Whig’s yells or any other things of
that sort, the Great Northern Railway decided to nego-
tiate.

There were conferences between several members of
Parliament, including Mr. Jeremiah MacVeagh, who
has taken an active interest in this matter, and several
representatives of the Great Northern Railway. An ar-
rangement was come to subject to ratification by the
Directors, but now we read that the Directors refuse to
ratify the arrangement made by the Chairman and
Manager of the Railway. The resolution adopted by
the directors is all bluff. No doubt ever since the pub-
lication of the proposed arrangement the “true blues’’
have been making “signs’’ from the Freemason quar-
ters and passing resolutions in the Orange Lodges, and
so the Board of Directors ran away from the recent pro-

Is. Well, we are not altogether sorry. The inci-
dent will go to emphasise how little can be got from the
ascendancy except by fighting for it to the end.

A meoting of the Waterford Technical Committeo was
duly held on Friday last, but no definite result was
arrived at concorning the Headmastership to the Tech-
nical Schools. The committee mot to consider the capa-
bilitics of two candidates, Mr. Ferguson, from Scot-
land, and Mr. O’Shaughnessy, an Irishman whom, it

candidates were brought before the committee and ques-
tioned, but to the surprisc of all Mr. Ferguson 'wlt:h—

searching when they contemplate whether or not they
will buy an article of Irish manufacture. The
matter anyway was postponed until to-morrow (F11day),
when a final decision is to be arrived at. Besides Mr.
O’Shaughnessy there are in the field a Mr. Smith, a
Yorkshireman, and two gentlemen, Messrs. swobjohn
and Reynolds, who, we understand, are Scotch.

The mismanaged Rathmines Council, whose rates now

the township; some householders objected to the

ing guineas, as if thoy were rubbish, into the pockets

ablo little litigation over this public convenience; Mx.

sider the scanty evidence already given on the subject.
Mr. W. Spottiswood Green, the Head of the Fisheries
YBranch, undertook to explain, when giving his evidence

is that the value of fish landed on the coasts of England

~worth of fish, they would be landed; because the fish
‘landed in England came from various places, such as
:the coasts of Iceland, Portugal and the west coast of
“Treland.” He seems to argue that the reason why the
figures for Ireland are so small is not because our fishing
industry is undeveloped, but because all the fish taken
on the Trish coasts is not landed in Ireland, but a large
proportion is taken direct to England, where there is a
- greater demand. It cannot be denied that a great deal
- of the fish taken on the Irish coasts is landed in Eng-
land. But how much of it is taken by Irish fishermen?
" In order to prove that the Irish fishing industry is af least

of the fish landed on our shores should be in proportion

Now the population of Ireland is nearly one-seventh of
- that of England and Wales: therefore the value of the

Let us now apply Mr. Green’s principle to Scotland.
Tho Scotch, according to the Green standard, must be
tremendous fish eaters; for although their population
is only aboub the same as our own, they land nearly six
times as much fish on their coasts. And their fish eat-
ing powers must be growing, for we find in 1895 they
landed only £1,829,638 worth, but since then they have
been increasing the amount year by year until in 1904
they brought the total up to £2,307,902 for that year—
an increase of £478,264 over their total for 1895. And
probably the total for last year was still higher. Thus
wo find the annual increase which the Scotch have at-
tained in nine years is considerably more than our {otal
receipts at present. During tho same period, we also,
it is true, had an increase, but it amounted to only
£24,615 in 1904, as compared with an increase of
£478,264 for Scotland.

Here let me point out another flaw in Mr. Green’s
argument. He stated in explanation of the £6,000,000
worth of fish landed in England, that it came from
various places abroad, such as Iceland and Portugal.
He forgot—for surely as Head of the Fisheries Branch
he could not be ignorant of it—that the :£6,000,000 does
not include fish imported from abroad or landed by
foreign fishing vessels, nor even shell fish, which is a
very important item, amounting in value to several hun-
dred thousand pounds.

When asked by the Chairman of the Committee to
give the names of places where the Department gives
anstruction in fishing, Mr. Green mentioned only two—
and these so close to one another that they might almost
be considered one—Portrush and Portstewart in the
North. Why did he not mention other places? Is it
because there were no others to mention? And yet he
stated that “the need for instruction was principally
confined to the West of Ireland.”” It would be interest-
ing to know what the Department is doing for the fish-
eries of the West.

The Scotch have a Fishery Board which seems to have
a great deal more life in it than Mr. Green’s Fisheries
Branch. It was established by Act of Parliament in
1882. The Chairman of Committee asked Mr. Green
what he thought would be the effect of the creation of a
similar Fishery Board for Ireland, having no connection
with the Department. Mr. Green replied that the
Scottish Board was appointed by tho Crown, and he be-
lieved the Department with its several Boards was more
in consonance with Irish ideas.”” The “several *’ Boards
are only two—the Agricultural Board and the Board of
Technical Instruction—unless the Council of Agriculture
bo also considered a Board. There is no Fishery Board
under the Department, and if it is not in consonance
with Irish ideas to have a separate or independent Fish-
ery Board, there ought at least to be a distinet Boavd.
under the Department, devoted entirely to the interests
of our fisheries. The Iisheries Branch, which consists,
of Mr. Green and his staff, is ovidently not sufficient to
enablo us to develop our resources and keep pace with
our neighbours in this important industry.

the course of the inquiry the work done by Mr. Green’s

ing Board in Scotland. The Scottish Board have divided
the Scotch coasts for the purposes of fishery develop-
ment and administration into no less than twenty-six
districts, each of which has one or more fishery officers.
They have established an admirable system of organisa- .

ing to be saved from one’s friends, surely the editorials

teen thousand pounds lent by the Department, and who

THE CATHOLIC TRUTH SOCIETY.

I should like to make a few remarks in reference to
tho articlo last week of this newly self-constituted In-
spector of Catholic Truth Society hoxes, whose pere-
grinations brought him to Limerick lately. ~ Had he
morely stated facts without drawing inferences and
making contrasts, I should take no notice of his remarks,
espocially writing as he does over a nom-de-plume.

However, his nom-de-plume is only a very thin dis- b
guiso as far as I am concerned, and I may say that if,
instead of rushing into print, he had only mentioned
the matter to me when speaking to him on the Sunday =
of our Feis here (the Sunday in question, I presume),
he would have received the following explanation, as
far as our church is concerned.

Seeing my stock running out, I wrote on the previous
Tuesday to the office in Dublin for twenty-four dozen
copies of the C.T.S. publications. I have found the o
orders always punctually attended to, and counted on =~
this order arriving at farthest on Friday—the Friday E
before this awful visitation. Instead of that it did not 4
arrive till Tuesday. I believe the fault lay with the
railway people. 3

I have had charge of the box for about six months,
and have disposed of nearly 2,000 booklets. I attend to
the box as a rule every day, and try to keep it always
freshly supplied. With all due respect to our critic, I
think it is absurd to talk of putting twenty dozen in the
box. Lots of people, who never buy any of the books, 4
take them out for a few minutes, and throw them back A
any way; and if the box is packed full, they’ll stuff R
them in as best they can, soon making them  soiled 3
and dog-eared.”’ People will not pay even a penny for .
books that are soiled, and hence I find it much better to
keep from three to six copies in each compartment, and
renew them constantly. In this way the books are
always saleable, and, as a matter of fact, the box of
which I have charge has well paid for itself.

I wonder has our Irish-Ireland critic remarked what
a distaste the public have for purely Irish publications.
Of course, the number is limited, but such as they are,
they will not be bought. In fact the number all round
is very limited, and people tire of seeing the same books
constantly in the boxes.

I have greab respect for Ciapdn, and believe him to
be doing a man’s part in the Ivish-Ireland movement.
With his facility for writing and well-known powers of
research, may wo hope that he will soon add to our stock -
of useful C.I.S. publications. J. A. O’Connor.

The government of Ireland according to Irish ideas
goeth along right. merrily.  The police appear to be
supplying the ideas, and the people the Irish—on their
carts.

Under the auspices of the Right Honourable James
Bryce the reign of freedom began in Ireland, and the
people of that country have now obtained liberty not
to put their names on their carts in their own language.

It will be remembered that the King’s Speech at the
opening of Parliament promised some measure for asso-
ciating the people of Ireland more closely with the
government of their country. It is believed that this
will be achieved mainly through the agency of peelers,
prison warders, and income-tax collectors.

he did |
